coredata.dc:title: Label-free fluorescence turn-on strategy for trypsin activity based on thiolate-protected gold nanoclusters with bovine serum albumin as the substrate
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Dan
Chen, Chuanxia
Zhao, Jiahui
Sun, Jian
Yang, Xiurong
coredata.dc:description: By using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as the enzyme substrate, we propose a label-free, convenient, reliable and highly sensitive fluorescence “turn-on” assay for trypsin activity and inhibitor screening by means of fluorescent gold nanoclusters capped with 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (namely AuNCs@11-MUA) for the first time. The principle for the assay depends on the following features: (1) Cu2+-triggered fluorescence quenching of AuNCs owing to the binding between Cu2+ and the carboxyl groups, (2) the cleavage of BSA into amino acids/peptide fragments catalyzed by trypsin, and (3) the stronger binding ability between amino acids/peptide fragments and Cu2+ than that between AuNCs and Cu2+, accompanied by the conversion from fluorescence off-state to on-state. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed sensing assay shows a good linear relationship from 0.01 to 2μg/mL and provides an inspiring detection limit of 0.004μg/mL, which possesses adequate sensitivity for practical samples of human urine. In addition, our proposed trypsin bioassay can be expanded into the inhibitor evaluation by using trypsin inhibitor from soybean as a model. Such unprecedented AuNCs-based fluorescence “turn-on” analytical proposal for assessing trypsin activity gives a new sight to develop several other potential bioassays or enzyme activity assays with the help of different kinds of substrates in the near future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 11-Mercaptoundecanoic acid capped gold nanoclusters
Fluorescence turn-on
Trypsin activity
Inhibitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The fluorescence response of Cu2+ on AuNCs
Enzymatic assay for trypsin activity and trypsin inhibitor
Determination of trypsin in human urine samples
The quenching effect of Cu2+ on AuNCs@11-MUA
The sensing strategy for trypsin activity
The optimization of sensing conditions for trypsin activity
Fluorescence turn-on performance for trypsin activity
Detection of trypsin in human urine samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the fluorescent sensing of trypsin activity.

(A) The UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of AuNCs. Inset shows the photograph of the AuNCs under visible light (left) and 254nm UV light (right), respectively. (B) Typical TEM image of AuNCs. Inset shows the corresponding size distribution histogram.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of AuNCs in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ ions (top to bottom, 0–5μM) in 10mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH=8.0). (B) Plot of the F/F0 value of fluorescence at 608nm as a function of the concentration of Cu2+. (C) The liner responses of Cu2+ ranging from 0.2-1.5μM.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the sole AuNCs (1), AuNCs+Cu2+ (2), AuNCs+Cu2+ +BSA (3), AuNCs+Cu2+ +trypsin (4) and AuNCs+Cu2+ +BSA+trypsin (5) in 10mM Tris-HCl buffer at pH 8.0. Inset shows the corresponding photos under ultraviolet light. [Cu2+]=1.5μM; [BSA]=30μg/mL; [trypsin]=2.0μg/mL.
The effect of the concentration of BSA (A), pH (B), incubation time (C) and the concentration of CaCl2 (D) on the proposed assay.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the proposed sensing system toward different activities of trypsin, and (B) the plots of (F-F0)/F0 as a function of the concentrations of trypsin. Inset shows the liner responses to trypsin ranging from 0.01 to 2μg/mL. (C) (F-F0)/F0 of the proposed assay in addtion of the control enzymes (20μg/mL) with (red) and without (black) the coexistence of 2μg/mL trypsin after incubation for 60min. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of the proposed assay in the presence of different concentrations of trypsin inhibitor from soybean. (B) Inhibitory efficiency of soybean inhibitor on the activity of 2.0μg/mL trypsin under the optimized conditions. The error bar represents standard deviation of three replicate measurements.
Results of the determination of trypsin in human urine samples.

head.ce:title: Label-free fluorescence turn-on strategy for trypsin activity based on thiolate-protected gold nanoclusters with bovine serum albumin as the substrate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as the enzyme substrate, we propose a label-free, convenient, reliable and highly sensitive fluorescence “turn-on” assay for trypsin activity and inhibitor screening by means of fluorescent gold nanoclusters capped with 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (namely AuNCs@11-MUA) for the first time. The principle for the assay depends on the following features: (1) Cu2+-triggered fluorescence quenching of AuNCs owing to the binding between Cu2+ and the carboxyl groups, (2) the cleavage of BSA into amino acids/peptide fragments catalyzed by trypsin, and (3) the stronger binding ability between amino acids/peptide fragments and Cu2+ than that between AuNCs and Cu2+, accompanied by the conversion from fluorescence off-state to on-state. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed sensing assay shows a good linear relationship from 0.01 to 2μg/mL and provides an inspiring detection limit of 0.004μg/mL, which possesses adequate sensitivity for practical samples of human urine. In addition, our proposed trypsin bioassay can be expanded into the inhibitor evaluation by using trypsin inhibitor from soybean as a model. Such unprecedented AuNCs-based fluorescence “turn-on” analytical proposal for assessing trypsin activity gives a new sight to develop several other potential bioassays or enzyme activity assays with the help of different kinds of substrates in the near future.

ce:section.ce:para: Considerable attention has been drawn in the study of proteases due to their vital importance in regulating many essential physiological and pathological processes including digestion, blood coagulation, apoptosis, cancer, inflammation, neurodegenerative diseases, arthritis and atherosclerosis [1–3]. Therefore, they can be considered to be biological markers and therapeutic targets for these pathologies. Trypsin (EC 3.4.21.4), known as the most important digestive enzyme, is produced in the pancreas and specific for cleaving peptides mainly at the C-terminal side of arginine or lysine residues [4,5]. In addition, as a biomarker for pancreatitis, trypsin plays a key role in regulating pancreatic exocrine function [6]. The level of trypsin in biological fluids can serve as a reliable diagnostic indicator to distinguish acute pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis and pancreas transplant patients from healthy people [7]. All the abovementioned aspects underline the importance of trypsin detection.

Till now, a number of strategies have been established for the detection of trypsin, including electrochemistry [8], chromatography [9], fluorimetry [10–12], colorimetry [13,14] and surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [1]. Among them, fluorimetry, fantastically featuring with high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, simplicity and rapidity, has attracted considerable patience. Specially, the fluorescence turn-on strategies are of particular interest on account of their exceptional performances including less false signals, good selectivity and improved sensitivity [15,16]. Indeed, a few fluorescence turn-on assays for trypsin detection have been reported, and fluorometric indicators were mainly focused on fluorescent polymers [17], conjugated polyelectrolytes [18,19] and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [20]. However, certain drawbacks of traditional fluorescent probes, such as the rapid photobleaching, small Stokes shift and bad photostability, always limit the further applications. The harsh detection environment with added cytotoxic effect of the QDs-based assays should not be neglected. Therefore, the development of effective yet facile, sensitive and low toxic assays for sensing trypsin is still imperative and significant.

The development of new types of nanomaterials has witnessed the prosperity of fluorescent metal nanoclusters, which have been developed as a new class of fluorophores due to their incomparable features such as ultrasmall size (<2nm), low toxicity, excellent photostability and large Stokes shift [20,21]. Among them, thiolate-protected gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) stand out in terms of convenient synthesis route, preferable stability, good biocompatibility and tailorable surface properties [22,23]. These properties enable the development of thiolate-protected AuNCs-based fluorescent nanosensors for the detection of many biologically and environmentally important analytes by monitoring the changes of their fluorescence intensity [24–26]. More impressively, by utilizing of the “turn-off-on” process of AuNCs, several fluorescent assays have been successfully established for enzyme activity evaluation like pyrophosphatase [27], acetylcholinesterase (AChE) [28], phospholipase C (PLC) [29] and alkaline phosphatise (ALP) [30]. Therefore, the design of thiolate-protected AuNCs-based sensor for the detection of trypsin activity should be appealing.

Herein, we presented a novel, convenient, selective and sensitive fluorescence turn-on approach for monitoring trypsin activity based on AuNCs by employing bovine serum albumin (BSA) as the substrate. The fluorescent AuNCs capped with 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (namely AuNCs@11-MUA) abundant in carboxyl groups were firstly prepared by convenient one-pot synthetic strategy. Upon treatment with Cu2+, effective charge transfer from AuNCs to Cu2+ took place because of the combination of Cu2+ and carboxyl groups, and the strong fluorescence of AuNCs@11-MUA was found to be effectively quenched simultaneously. Under the specific catalysis of trypsin, BSA was cleaved into amino acids or peptide fragments, which could strongly chelate with Cu2+ to form more stable complexes. Thus, Cu2+ was liberated from the AuNCs@11-MUA/Cu2+ ensemble and the quenched fluorescence could be restored in respond to trypsin activity. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of the AuNCs/Cu2+ ensemble-based “switch-on” fluorescent probe for trypsin sensing. By taking advantage of the competitive reaction between Cu2+, AuNCs and the enzyme-generated substances, our developed method possesses many merits, including unambiguous sensing mechanism, rapidity, simplicity, low cost, high sensitivity and excellent selectivity.

Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (Ⅲ) trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (11-MUA), CuSO4·5H2O, NaOH, tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), trypsin (bovine pancrease), trypsin inhibitor (from soybean), BSA, AChE (from Electrophorus electricus), ALP, glucose oxidase (GOx), endonuclease I (ECoR I), lysozyme, papain and pepsin were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich(USA). Other inorganic salts were bought from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. (China). The ultrapure water (18.2MΩ) was used throughout all the experiments.

UV-vis and fluorescence measurements were recorded on a Cary 50 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian) and a Hitachi F-4600 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo), respectively. Photographs were taken with a commercial digital camera. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out by using a Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV.

All glasswares used in the preparation were soaked in aqua regia and rinsed thoroughly with ultrapure water before use. We prepared fluorescent AuNCs by using 11-MUA as both capping agent and reductant following a one-pot method described in the published protocol with minor modifications [27]. In brief, 11-MUA (26.2mg) and NaOH (0.3mL, 1M) were dispersed in 18.2mL of water to form a uniform solution. Freshly prepared HAuCl4 (1.5mL, 20mM) was quickly injected into the above aqueous solution Then the mixture was maintained for 5h without stirring at room temperature, during which the solution remained colorless all the time. Afterward, the transparent solution was subjected to dialysis with a 8–14kDa cut-off dialysis bag for 24h to get rid of the impurities. The resulting purified AuNCs was stored in the refrigerator at 4°C for further characterization and use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence response of Cu2+ on AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared AuNCs solution was diluted 5 times, named AuNCs solution (1/5) in the Tris-HCl buffer (10mM, pH 8.0). To evaluate the quenching effect of Cu2+ on the fluorescence of AuNCs@11-MUA, 60μL of AuNCs@11-MUA (1/5), 890μL of Tris-HCl (10mM, pH 8.0) and 50μL of various concentrations of Cu2+ were sequentially added to a series of centrifuge tubes. The mixture was equilibrated for 2min at room temperature to measure the fluorescence emission spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzymatic assay for trypsin activity and trypsin inhibitor
ce:section.ce:para: For trypsin activity, 50μL of trypsin solutions with various concentrations, 30μL of BSA (1mg/mL) and 20μL of CaCl2 (1mM) were added into 790μL of 10mM Tris-HCl at pH 8.0 in order. The mixture was incubated at 37°C for 60min. Finally, the mixture containing 60μL of AuNCs@11-MUA (1/5) and 50μL of Cu2+ (30μM) was introduced into the system. And the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture were measured with 1cm cuvette after 2min.

For trypsin inhibitor screening, 50μL of trypsin (40μg/mL) was initially mixed with 20μL of different concentrations of trypsin inhibitor at 37°C for 15min. Then 30μL of BSA (1mg/mL), 20μL of CaCl2 (1mM) and 770μL of 10mM Tris-HCl at pH 8.0 were added in order and incubated at 37°C for 60min. After that, the mixture containing 60μL of AuNCs@11-MUA (1/5) and 50μL of Cu2+ (30μM) was introduced to each mixture. Two minutes later, fluorescence spectra were recorded. The inhibition efficiency (IE) was determined by the following equation:
(1) IE (%)=100×(F−Fi)/(F−F0)
Where F0 stands for the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs/Cu2+-BSA ensemble, F stands for the fluorescence intensity with trypsin, and Fi stands for that with both trypsin and inhibitor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of trypsin in human urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers and were analyzed after being centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min. Then the supernatant urine samples were diluted 10 times with ultrapure water before detection. 30μL of BSA (1mg/mL), 20μL of CaCl2 (1mM) and 200μL of diluted urine samples were added into 590μL of 10mM Tris-HCl buffer solutions at pH 8.0 in order. Then 50μL of different concentrations of trypsin standards were spiked into the above solutions. Following the procedure of enzymatic assay for trypsin activity, the emission fluorescence at 608nm was measured with 1cm cuvette.

Through the direct reduction of HAuCl4 in alkaline aqueous solutions at room temperature, AuNCs were easily prepared, which emitted bright orange red fluorescence under 254nm UV light irradiation while appearing nearly colorless under visible light (inset of Fig. 1 A). UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were investigated to study the optical properties of the as-prepared AuNCs. Learned from the black line in Fig. 1A, AuNCs exhibited a strong but featureless absorption at 236nm in the UV region but no apparent absorption in the visible region. This phenomenon was ascribed to the molecule-like properties of the AuNCs and the lack of larger plasmonic gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [31,32]. The maximum emission intensity was achieved at 608nm upon excitation at 257nm (the blue and red line in Fig. 1A). The particle size and morphology of the AuNCs were characterized using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1B, the AuNCs were well-dispersed and displayed a narrow diameter distribution in the range from 0.75 to 2.55nm with an average size of about 1.68nm. We noticed that the fluorescence of AuNCs was highly pH dependent (Fig. S1A). The results showed that the fluorescence of AuNCs was rather low in acidic solutions, probably due to the protonation of carboxyl groups on the surface [26]. By comparison, the fluorescence intensity remained high in neutral and alkaline conditions (pH of 6–10). In addition, the as-obtained AuNCs can maintain relative stability for 1 month (Fig. S1B), which benefited the further development of AuNCs applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching effect of Cu2+ on AuNCs@11-MUA
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that the thiolate-capped AuNCs could be served as fluorescence signal transducer for chemsensing of several metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+ and Ag+) based on the propensity of fluorescence quenching [33,34]. Cu2+ is one of the best known fluorescence quenchers due to its intrinsic paramagnetic properties and strong tendency to chelate special carboxyl group [32,35]. Therefore, it is expected that mixing of Cu2+ with AuNCs could result in the binding of Cu2+ with the carboxyl groups of 11-MUA and efficaciously fluorescence quenching by charge transfer from AuNCs to Cu2+ [36,37]. We further assessed the influence of a variety of metal ions (Cu2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ag+ and Hg2+) on the fluorescence responses of AuNCs@11-MUA. As can be seen from Fig. S2, no obvious fluorescence changes were observed in the presence of other metal ions and only Cu2+ showed remarkable fluorescence quenching effect. Fig. 2 A displays the gradual decrease of the fluorescence of AuNCs@11-MUA with the Cu2+ concentrations increasing from 0 to 5μM. As the concentration of Cu2+ increased, the quenching effect of the AuNCs was progressively reinforced. A good linear relationship was found between F/F0 (where F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensities in the presence or absence of Cu2+, respectively) and the concentration of Cu2+ ranging from 0.2-1.5μM (Fig. 2C). An ideal fluorescence quenching efficiency (∼88%) was observed upon addition of 1.5μM Cu2+, and this concentration would be adopted in view of the high sensitivity of the subsequent turn-on analysis. Fig. S3 elucidates the time dependence of fluorescence intensity of the mixture of AuNCs and Cu2+. It was noted that the fluorescence of AuNCs decreased dramatically in the first 1min and tended to reach saturation at 2min, proving the fast response of the proposed sensor. Furthermore, the Cu2+-triggered fluorescence quenching could be almost completely restored upon addition of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) (a stronger chelator for Cu2+) (Fig. S4). This result implies that the interaction between Cu2+ and AuNCs is reversible and inspires us to develop competitive assay between AuNCs, Cu2+ and target analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing strategy for trypsin activity
ce:section.ce:para: Incorporating 11-MUA as both a reducing and capping agent not only has superiorities of facility and convenience, but also endows the as-prepared AuNCs with large amounts of carboxyl groups on the surface and strong fluorescence. Scheme 1 demonstrates the detection strategy for trypsin activity based on the fluorescence turn-off-on process with the assistance of Cu2+ and BSA. Abundance in carboxyl groups of AuNCs enables them to coordinate with Cu2+ and the as-formed AuNCs@11-MUA/Cu2+ ensemble might block the Au-S charge transfer in the interface of AuNCs. Significant fluorescence quenching could be thus observed, which could be attributed to the occurrence of non-radiative charge transfer from AuNCs to Cu2+ [37,38]. It has been reported that BSA is a well-known protease substrate with very weak interaction with Cu2+ [18]. Trypsin will cleave BSA on the C-terminal side of lysine and arginine amino acid residues to expose more binding sites inside the BSA [18,39,40]. The as-produced amino acids or peptide fragments have robust affinity for Cu2+ to form more stable complexes[41–43], which disturbs the combination of Cu2+ and AuNCs@11-MUA. As a result, the charge transfer from AuNCs@11-MUA to Cu2+ will be impeded and AuNCs@11-MUA will emit intense fluorescence again. Based on this feature, excellent analytical performance for this fluorescent “turn-on” assay for trypsin activity could be anticipated.

Following the design, fluorescence measurements were carried out to confirm the feasibility of the target-ruled competitive assay based on AuNCs/Cu2+ system. Fig. 3 depicts the typical fluorescence response of this trypsin assay. It was obviously seen that the presence of 1.5μM Cu2+ could cause a significant fluorescence quenching of AuNCs by the effective charge transfer (curve 2 versus 1, Fig. 3). The addition of individual BSA or trypsin could not exert any obvious impact on the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs/Cu2+ (curve 3 and 4, Fig. 3). Significantly, in the presence of both BSA and trypsin, the trypsin-catalyzed reaction could lead to a substantial enhanced fluorescence readout, which was 5.64 fold as high as that of AuNCs/Cu2+ (curve 5, Fig. 3). This was reasonable, since in global BSA there is only one binding site for Cu2+ and this interaction is too weak to withdraw Cu2+ from the AuNCs/Cu2+ ensemble [18,44]. However, the amino acids or peptide fragments liberated from the cleaving of BSA by trypsin have stronger affinity with Cu2+, and could snatch Cu2+ from AuNCs/Cu2+ system, thus resetting the fluorescence of AuNCs. The evolution of the fluorescence under UV light also clearly showed this “off-on” process (the inset of Fig. 3). Subsequently, some control experiments were undertaken to further demonstrate our proposal (Fig. S5). It was noticeable that negligible alteration in the fluorescence of the sole AuNCs without Cu2+ was observed in the presence of BSA, trypsin or BSA and trypsin together, indicating the lack of direct interactions between these substances and AuNCs@11-MUA. This result further revealed that it is the robust affinity between the generated amino acids or peptide fragments with Cu2+ could lead to the marvelous fluorescent enhancement. Based on the aforementioned observations, the fluorescence “turn-on” assay for sensing trypsin could be designed and established based on Cu2+-triggered non-fluorescence state and trypsin enzymolysis of BSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization of sensing conditions for trypsin activity
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the extreme detection capability of the proposed sensor, the concentration of BSA, pH, incubation time and the concentration of activator (CaCl2) were dissected in detail and optimized. We used the fluorescence enhancement efficiency, that was (F-F0)/F0 as a criterion to appraise the sensing performance, where F and F0 corresponded to the fluorescence intensity in the prescence and absence of trypsin, respectively.

The concentration of BSA plays an important role in the detection of trypsin. It has been reported that BSA only has one binding site for Cu2+ with a relatively weak interaction [18]. Obviously, the increase in the concentration of BSA gave rise to more amino acids or peptide fragments, thereby higher fluorescence responses. However, a superfluous concentration would bring out a higher blank signal, thus decreasing the detection sensitivity. Fig. 4 A signifies that the fluorescence intensities of the AuNCs@11-MUA/Cu2+ ensemble in the absence and presence of trypsin both progressively increased with the increase of BSA concentrations ranging from 0 to 60μg/mL. Significantly, the maximum fluorecence enhancement efficiency (F-F0)/F0 was observed when the concentration of BSA was 30μg/mL. Therefore, 30μg/mL BSA was chosen as the optimal substrate concentration in the sensing system. As mentioned before, the fluorescence of the AuNCs@11-MUA was strong and stable when the pH was above 6.0, besides, the trypsin activity was relatively low below pH 7.0 because the reported optimal pH range for trypsin was 7.5–8.5 [10,11]. Thus, the effect of pH in the range of 6.0-10.0 was investigated and the results were elucidated in Fig. 4B. At alkaline pH values (pH>9), Cu2+ always existed in a hydroxide form Cu(OH)2, thus remarkably depressing the quenching effect on AuNCs@11-MUA[35]. Besides, when the pH was above 7.0, the activity of trypsin was effectively activated. And for (F-F0)/F0, it increased with pH increasing from 6.0 to 8.0, and then decreased with pH from 8.0 to 10.0. So pH 8.0 was set in the following experiments. It should be noteworthy that the incubation time was also crucial in this study. Fig. 4C reveals the fluorescence responses of the proposed assay in the presence of different concentrations of trypsin. The fluorometric signals gradually increased with the enzymatic reaction time and reached a plateau after about 60min, implying the complete hydrolysis of BSA by trypsin. By compromising the enhancement effect and time consumption, 60min was chosen as the incubation time.

More importantly and impressively, Ca2+ was suggested to play an important role in the enzymatic reaction. It has been reported that certain concentration of Ca2+ could effectively activate the trypsin activity and increase its stability against autolysis [45,46]. Then we assessed the influence of different concentrations of Ca2+ on our assay system. The results in Fig. 4D demonstrated that the trypsin activity could be gradually enhanced by increasing the concentration of CaCl2. However, a higher concentration of CaCl2 (>20μM) would lead to great enhancement on the fluorescence of AuNCs. Comprehensively considering the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs and the enhancement of enzymatic hydrolysis by the addition of CaCl2, we chose 20μM as the optimum concentraion of CaCl2 in our detection experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence turn-on performance for trypsin activity
ce:section.ce:para: Trypsin could efficiently catalyze the hydrolysis of BSA. It was foreseeable that the addition of trypsin could lead to the breakdown of the AuNCs/Cu2+ ensemble, thus recovering the fluorescence of AuNCs. Under the optimized conditions discussed above, the fluorescence spectra of AuNCs/Cu2+-BSA system upon introduction with trypsin of different concentrations from 0 to 10μg/mL were recorded (Fig. 5 A). As designed, we found continual increase of the fluorescence from the non-fluorescence state with increase of the added trypsin, implying a gradual hydrolysis of BSA. The plot of fluorescence enhancement efficiency versus the concentration with a plateau point at 10μg/mL was displayed in Fig. 5B. In this regard, there was a quasi-linear relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and trypsin concentrations over the range from 0.01 to 2μg/mL, where the fitted liner equation could be described as (F-F0)/F0 =−0.0598+1.857 [trypsin] (μg/mL), R2 =0.996. The proposed sensing platform afforded a satisfactory detection limit of 0.004μg/mL, which was comparable or superior to most of the previously reported trypsin assays listed in Table S1. To explore the specificity of the fluorescence “turn-on” assay toward trypsin, we treated our system with seven other enzymes, including ALP, GOx, AChE, ECoR I, lysozyme, papain and pepsin (Fig. 5C). Although the concentrations of these non-specific enzymes were much higher than that of trypsin, none of them could evoke a convincing fluorescence response as trypsin did. The coexistence of the control enzymes with trypsin also did not interfere in the recognition of trypsin. This could be explained by the high specific hydrolysis of BSA catalyzed by trypsin, manifesting excellent selectivity of the nanosensor toward tryspin detection. Furthermore, the fluorescence response toward 0.4, 1, 2 and 4μg/mL trypsin of the proposed sensor was recorded using aged AuNCs after storage at 4°C for 4 weeks (Fig. S6A). The results showed negligible changes after storage (Fig. S6B), demonstrating good reproducibility of the proposed sensor.

The screening of protease inhibitors is of great importance for potential drug development. Lack of trypsin inhibitor will cause the hypertrophy or hyperplasia of pancreas, leading to the disorder of digestive function, or even dizziness, nausea, diarrhea and other diseases [47,48]. The described method can also be used to screen trypsin inhibitors and the cleavage of BSA could be retarded by inactivating trypsin with corresponding inhibitors. We tested this AuNCs/Cu2+-BSA system against a typical trypsin inhibitor from soybean. The control experiments have ascertained that the addition of trypsin inhibitor from soybean could not significantly influence the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs (data not shown). So the fluorescence spectra of AuNCs containing 2.0μg/mL trypsin upon treatment with different concentrations of trypsin inhibitor were recorded (Fig. 6 A). Addition of trypsin inhibitor from soybean could effectively inhibit the trypsin activity and the fluorescence progressively decreased with increasing concentrations of trypsin inhibitor. On the basis of the plot of IE versus the concentraion of trypsin inhibitor from soybean (Fig. 6B), the IC50 value (the inhibitory concentration required for 50% inhibition of the enzyme activity) was estimated to be 372ng/mL. The results clearly showed that the current method could not only be used for probing protease activity but also for the screening of potential protease inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of trypsin in human urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the potential application of our fluorescence “turn-on” assay in clinical diagnosis, we applied it to determinate the content of trypsin in human urines. The two samples were found to be free from trypsin, which agreed well with the finding that there is virtually no trypsin in urine of healthy persons with other developed tryspin sensors [10,46,49]. The results obtained by the standard addition experiments were summarized in Table 1 . It was noted that the developed sensing assay yielded satisfactory recoveries within the range of 91.4%-98.2% with the relative standrad deviations (RSDs) ranging from 1.39% to 3.02%. This unambiguously demonstrated that the AuNCs/Cu2+-BSA ensemble remained high trypsin sensing ability, even in complex matrix, and therefore had potential applicability in facile and rapid cilnical diagnosis with high accuracy and reliability.

In summary, we proposed a novel and reliable fluorescence assay for trypsin activity and its inhibitor screening, wherein thiolate-protected AuNCs and BSA served as the signal reporter and enzyme substrate, respectively. By taking advantage of the trypsin-regulated competitive reaction between Cu2+, AuNCs@11-MUA and the generated amino acids or peptide fragments of the trypsin-catalyzed hydrolysis, this presented assay allows the sensitive detection of trypsin activity through the fluorescence “off-on” process. The high specific catalysis of enzymes provides excellent selectivity for sensing trypsin. Furthermore, the descried system can be applied to evaluate the trypsin activity in human urine samples and to screen the inhibitor of trypsin. As far as we know, this is the first time to propose a sensor for trypsin based on Cu2+-triggered fluorescence quenching/recovery of AuNCs. Given its simplicity, flexibility, ease of operation and unequivocal sensing principle, we envision that this study may open a new avenue to generate other enzyme assays in a similar fashion by using other substrates. Meanwhile, our work also shows great potential of AuNCs in constructing other biosensing platforms for various compounds.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21435005, 21627808 and 21605139).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.03.031.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive surface-enhanced Raman scattering detection of trypsin based on anti-aggregation of 4-mercaptopyridine-functionalized silver nanoparticles: an optical sensing platform toward proteases
Fluorescence anisotropy assay for the traceless kinetic analysis of protein digestion
An autoinhibited coiled-coil design strategy for split-protein protease sensors
Chemiluminescence flow-through biosensor for glucose with eggshell membrane as enzyme immobilization platform
Trypsin cleaves exclusively C-terminal to arginine and lysine residues
Efficient and specific trypsin digestion of microgram to nanogram quantities of proteins in organic-aqueous solvent systems
FRET-based modified graphene quantum dots for direct trypsin quantification in urine
Sensitive cell apoptosis assay based on caspase-3 activity detection with graphene oxide-assisted electrochemical signal amplification
Recognition of trypsin autolysis products by high-performance liquid chromatography and mass spectrometry
Highly sensitive fluorescent detection of trypsin based on BSA-stabilized gold nanoclusters
An ultra-sensitive and label-free fluorescent probe for trypsin and inhibitor based on DNA hosted Cu nanoclusters
A label-free fluorescence assay for trypsin based on the electron transfer between oligonucleotide-stabilized Ag nanoclusters and cytochrome c
Highly sensitive, label-free colorimetric assay of trypsin using silver nanoparticles
A new label-free continuous fluorometric assay for trypsin and inhibitor screening with tetraphenylethene compounds
A carbon dots-based fluorescence turn-on method for DNA determination
A unique turn-on fluorescence signalling strategy for highly specific detection of ascorbic acid using carbon dots as sensing probe
Label-free and real-time monitoring of trypsin activity in living cells by quantum-dot-based fluorescent sensors
Label-free, homogeneous, and fluorescence turn-on detection of protease using conjugated polyelectrolytes
A fluorescence turn on trypsin assay based on aqueous polyfluorene
A novel optical nanoprobe for trypsin detection and inhibitor screening based on Mn-doped ZnSe quantum dots
Fluorescence on-off responses of BSA-Cu system towards hydrogen peroxide and l-cysteine and their analysis applications
Fluorescent Au nanoclusters: recent progress and sensing applications
Correlating the crystal structure of a thiol-protected Au25 cluster and optical properties
Water-soluble gold nanoclusters prepared by protein-ligand interaction as fluorescent probe for real-time assay of pyrophosphatase activity
Applications of metal nanoclusters in environmental monitoring
11-Mercaptoundecanoic acid directed one-pot synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent gold nanoclusters and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of Cr3+ and Cr6+
Highly sensitive real-time assay of inorganic pyrophosphatase activity based on the fluorescent gold nanoclusters
Selective and sensitive detection of acetylcholinesterase activity using denatured protein-protected gold nanoclusters as a label-free probe
Synthesis of fluorescent gold nanodot-liposome hybrids for detection of phospholipase C and its inhibitor
Switchable fluorescence of gold nanoclusters for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase and its application in immunoassay
Dithiothreitol-capped ﬂuorescent gold nanoclusters: An efﬁcient probe for detection of copper(II) ions in aqueous solution, Biosens
Electrochemical immunosensor for detection of E. coli in urban sludge based on dendrimer-encapsulated Au and enhanced gold nanoparticle labels
Microwave-assisted rapid synthesis of luminescent gold nanoclusters for sensing Hg2+ in living cells using fluorescence imaging
Facile synthesis of enhanced fluorescent gold-silver bimetallic nanocluster and its application for highly sensitive detection of inorganic pyrophosphatase activity
Integrated logic gate for fluorescence turn-on detection of histidine and cysteine based on Ag/Au bimetallic nanoclusters-Cu2+ Ensemble
Synthesis of novel water-soluble silicon quantum dots with imidazole groups and its application in fluorescent detection of trace copper in fruits and vegetables
Highly sensitive real-time assay of inorganic pyrophosphatase activity based on the fluorescent gold nanoclusters
Colorimetric sensing of α-Amino Acids and its application for the label-rree detection of protease
γ-Oxo-1-pyrenebutyric acid used for fluorescent detection of serum albumins and trypsin
A green fluorescent chemosensor for amino acids provides a versatile high-throughput screening (HTS) assay for proteases
Fluorescence study of Cu2+-induced interaction between albumin and anionic polyelectrolytes
Self-powered sensor for naked-eye detection of serum trypsin
A sensitive and label-free trypsin colorimetric sensor with cytochrome c as a substrate
A soybean trypsin inhibitor. Crystallization and x-ray crystallographic study
Emerging principles in protease-based drug discovery
Urinary levels of activated trypsin in whole-organ pancreas transplant patients with duodenocystostomies

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dan Zhao received her BS and MS degree from Central South University and Shenyang Institute of Applied Ecology, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2010 and 2013, respectively. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D degree under the supervision of Prof. Xiurong Yang in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her research interests focus mainly on the construction of novel nanomaterials-based optical sensors for enzyme activity assaying.

Chuanxia Chen obtained her BS degree in 2011 from Shandong Agricultural University, China. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D degree under the supervision of Prof. Xiurong Yang in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her current research is the synthesis of functional nanomaterials and their chemosensors application.

Jiahui Zhao obtained her BS and MS degree from Nanjing University and Jilin University in 2013 and 2016, respectively. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D degree under the supervision of Prof. Xiurong Yang in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her research interests focus mainly on the synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials and their application in bioanalysis.

Jian Sun is an associate professor of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research is mainly focused on the fabrication of biosensors.

Xiurong Yang is a professor of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her current research includes study of biomolecules interaction, fabrication of biosensors, and synthesis of functional namomaterials.
